---
title: "Lecture 2: The Histogram That Was Too Wide"
subtitle: "Estimation in High Dimensions"
date: "2026-05-15"
format:
  html:
    toc: true
    toc-depth: 2
    number-sections: false
    code-fold: false
    code-tools: false
    theme: cosmo
  pdf:
    toc: true
    toc-depth: 2
    number-sections: false
    fig-pos: 'H'
---

```{python}
#| label: setup
#| echo: false
#| warning: false
import numpy as np
import pandas as pd
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
from scipy import stats
from scipy.stats import norm

rng = np.random.default_rng(2026)
plt.rcParams.update({
    "figure.figsize": (8, 4),
    "font.size": 11,
    "axes.spines.top": False,
    "axes.spines.right": False,
})

# Compute gene-wise t-statistics, p-values, and z-scores on the Golub data,
# once at top of essay so subsequent figures can reuse the variables.
expr = pd.read_csv("../data/golub_expression.csv", index_col=0)
meta = pd.read_csv("../data/golub_metadata.csv")
subtype = meta["subtype"].values
all_mask = subtype == "ALL"
aml_mask = subtype == "AML"

t_stats, golub_pvals = stats.ttest_ind(
    expr.loc[:, all_mask].values, expr.loc[:, aml_mask].values, axis=1, equal_var=False
)
# Two-sided z-scores carrying direction of effect
golub_z = norm.ppf(1 - golub_pvals / 2) * np.sign(t_stats)
golub_z = golub_z[np.isfinite(golub_z)]
n_genes = len(golub_pvals)
```

## Orientation

Lecture 1 used the $p$-value histogram to *decide* which genes to call discoveries. This lecture uses the same picture to *estimate* quantities directly from it: the proportion of true nulls $\pi_0$, the density $f_1$ of the alternatives, and — for any specific test — the posterior probability that its null hypothesis is true. The unifying framework is Efron's two-groups model,

$$f(z) = \pi_0\, f_0(z) + (1-\pi_0)\, f_1(z),$$

a finite mixture that subsumes every procedure developed in Lecture 1 and produces, as a bonus, a per-test posterior — the local false discovery rate — that classical testing cannot. The historical anchor for the approach is Stein's 1956 inadmissibility result: in the parallel-estimation problem that high-dimensional data create, the natural estimator is not optimal, and the aggregate of measurements can be used to improve each individual one. The Singh prostate-cancer microarray ([Singh et al., 2002](https://doi.org/10.1016/S1535-6108(02)00030-2)) is the running cautionary example: an empirical $z$-score histogram visibly wider than $N(0,1)$, signaling that the theoretical null distribution is wrong and that the empirical null must replace it before any inference is trustworthy. The lecture closes with a bridge to Day 2, which continues the estimation story in the geometry of dimension reduction (Lecture 3) and the regularized regression of $p \gg n$ models (Lecture 4).

---

## Many parameters, one dataset

Classical statistics develops estimators one parameter at a time. The mean of a sample is estimated from that sample; the regression coefficient for a predictor is estimated from the data on that predictor. Each estimate is a function of its own data, treated as independent of every other estimate the analyst might compute.

High-dimensional inference inverts that posture. The same dataset that produces an estimate for gene 4,237 produces estimates for genes 1 through 4,236 and for genes 4,238 through 10,000. The 9,999 other estimates are not independent of the one of interest; collectively, they describe the *population* of estimates and constrain what any single one can plausibly be.

The classical estimator discards this information. Each test is conducted in isolation; each $p$-value is computed only from the data for its own gene. The estimation perspective on multiple testing recognizes that the histogram of $p$-values — or, equivalently, the histogram of $z$-scores — is itself an estimable object, and that estimating it answers questions the classical procedure cannot.

That is the program of this lecture.

---

## Stein's paradox and the case for shrinkage

The historical anchor is [Stein's (1956)](https://projecteuclid.org/proceedings/berkeley-symposium-on-mathematical-statistics-and-probability/Proceedings-of-the-Third-Berkeley-Symposium-on-Mathematical-Statistics-and/Chapter/Inadmissibility-of-the-Usual-Estimator-for-the-Mean-of-a/bsmsp/1200501656) result on the inadmissibility of the sample mean. Suppose one observes $y_1, \ldots, y_d$ with $y_i \sim N(\theta_i, 1)$ and wishes to estimate the vector $\boldsymbol\theta = (\theta_1, \ldots, \theta_d)$ under squared-error loss. The natural estimator is $\hat\theta_i = y_i$. Stein proved that this estimator is *inadmissible* whenever $d \geq 3$: there exists another estimator with strictly smaller risk for every $\boldsymbol\theta$. The [James–Stein estimator (1961)](https://projecteuclid.org/proceedings/berkeley-symposium-on-mathematical-statistics-and-probability/Proceedings-of-the-Fourth-Berkeley-Symposium-on-Mathematical-Statistics-and/Chapter/Estimation-with-Quadratic-Loss/bsmsp/1200512173) realizes the improvement explicitly,

$$\hat\theta_i^{\mathrm{JS}} = \bar y + \left(1 - \frac{(d-3)\sigma^2}{\sum_j (y_j - \bar y)^2}\right)(y_i - \bar y),$$

shrinking each coordinate toward the grand mean of all coordinates. The shrinkage factor is data-dependent: when the $y_i$ are tightly clustered, more shrinkage; when they are spread out, less. The dependence of the estimate for $\theta_i$ on the other coordinates is what produces the risk improvement.

```{python}
#| label: fig-js-shrinkage
#| echo: false
#| fig-cap: "James–Stein shrinkage in action. Twenty true means $\\theta_i$ are drawn from $N(0,1)$; each is observed once with unit noise, yielding $y_i \\sim N(\\theta_i, 1)$. The MLE puts $\\hat\\theta_i = y_i$ (top, large dots). The James–Stein estimator shrinks each $y_i$ toward the grand mean $\\bar y$ by the data-dependent factor in the JS formula (bottom, small dots, with arrows showing the shrinkage). The total squared error is reduced: under repeated simulations, JS averages a 35–45% lower sum of squared errors than the MLE for this regime."
#| fig-height: 4
sim_rng = np.random.default_rng(42)
d = 20
sigma = 1.0
theta = sim_rng.normal(0, 1, size=d)
y = theta + sim_rng.normal(0, sigma, size=d)
ybar = y.mean()
ss = np.sum((y - ybar) ** 2)
c = max(0.0, 1 - (d - 3) * sigma ** 2 / ss)
theta_js = ybar + c * (y - ybar)

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(8.5, 4))
order = np.argsort(theta)
xs = np.arange(d)

# MLE points (top row) and JS points (bottom row)
y_pos_mle, y_pos_js = 1.0, 0.0
ax.scatter(xs, np.full(d, y_pos_mle), s=60, color="#444",
           label=r"MLE $\hat\theta_i = y_i$", zorder=3)
ax.scatter(xs, np.full(d, y_pos_js), s=60, color="#c0504d",
           label=r"James–Stein $\hat\theta_i^{\,\mathrm{JS}}$", zorder=3)

# Annotate each point with its value to make shrinkage visible
for j in range(d):
    ax.text(xs[j], y_pos_mle + 0.15, f"{y[order][j]:+.1f}",
            ha="center", va="bottom", fontsize=7, color="#444")
    ax.text(xs[j], y_pos_js - 0.15, f"{theta_js[order][j]:+.1f}",
            ha="center", va="top", fontsize=7, color="#c0504d")
    ax.annotate(
        "", xy=(xs[j], y_pos_js + 0.05), xytext=(xs[j], y_pos_mle - 0.05),
        arrowprops=dict(arrowstyle="-|>", color="#888", lw=0.7),
    )

ax.axhline(y_pos_js, color="#c0504d", alpha=0.15, lw=0.8)
ax.axhline(y_pos_mle, color="#444", alpha=0.15, lw=0.8)
ax.set_xticks([])
ax.set_yticks([])
ax.set_xlim(-0.8, d - 0.2)
ax.set_ylim(-0.6, 1.6)
ax.set_title(f"Each MLE is pulled toward $\\bar y = {ybar:+.2f}$ by shrinkage factor $c = {c:.2f}$")
ax.legend(loc="upper left", frameon=False, fontsize=9)
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()
```

The Stein result was counterintuitive in 1956 and remains so. Each $y_i$ is the maximum-likelihood estimate of $\theta_i$, depends only on the data relevant to $\theta_i$, and is unbiased. None of that survives the move to vector loss: when one cares about all of the $\theta_i$ simultaneously, the estimator can be strictly improved by allowing each coordinate to depend on the others.

[Efron and Morris (1977)](https://www.jstor.org/stable/24953895) demonstrated the practical force of the result with a baseball illustration. End-of-season batting averages for 18 major-league players are predicted more accurately by *shrinking each player's early-season average toward the grand mean across players* than by using each player's own data alone. The players are independent in any biological or causal sense; their data nevertheless improve every individual estimate.

### The Efron–Morris baseball analysis in detail

Efron and Morris selected 18 major-league batters from the 1970 season — all the players who had recorded exactly 45 at-bats by a fixed date in early May. Each player's batting average over those first 45 at-bats was treated as $y_i$, the noisy observation. The true skill of each player was estimated to be his batting average over the *remainder* of the season (roughly 400 additional at-bats), which served as $\theta_i$. The question: given $y_1, \ldots, y_{18}$, which estimator best predicts $\theta_1, \ldots, \theta_{18}$?

Three candidate estimators were compared.

- **MLE:** $\hat\theta_i^{\mathrm{MLE}} = y_i$. Each prediction is the player's own early-season average.
- **Grand mean:** $\hat\theta_i^{\mathrm{GM}} = \bar y$. Predict the league-wide early-season average for every player; ignore individual data entirely.
- **James–Stein:** $\hat\theta_i^{\mathrm{JS}} = \bar y + c\,(y_i - \bar y)$, with $c$ the data-dependent shrinkage factor of the previous equation. Each prediction is a convex combination of the player's own average and the grand mean.

The grand mean and the MLE bracket the two extremes — full pooling and no pooling — and JS is between them.

The result, measured by total squared prediction error against the end-of-season averages, was that James–Stein dominated the MLE by a factor of more than three; it outperformed the MLE on 15 of the 18 individual players. The grand mean estimator was worst overall but, for a few players whose early averages were unusually extreme, came closer to their true skill than the MLE did. The lesson is not that JS is always best for every player — Roberto Clemente, who batted .400 over his first 45 at-bats and ended the season at .346, was over-shrunk by JS — but that JS is better *on aggregate*, and on aggregate is what total squared loss measures.

```{python}
#| label: fig-baseball-sim
#| echo: false
#| fig-cap: "A simulated version of the Efron–Morris baseball analysis. Eighteen true batting averages $\\theta_i$ are drawn around a league mean of 0.265 with reasonable spread; each player's early-season average $y_i$ is simulated as 45 Bernoulli trials at $\\theta_i$ (left panel). The three estimators (MLE = early average; grand mean = league average for everyone; James–Stein = shrinkage between the two) are evaluated against the true $\\theta_i$ (right panel). For this simulation JS wins on aggregate squared loss while losing to the MLE on the two extreme players (the very best and very worst hitters). The qualitative pattern matches Efron & Morris (1977)."
#| fig-height: 4
sim_rng2 = np.random.default_rng(1970)
n_players, at_bats = 18, 45
theta_b = np.clip(sim_rng2.normal(0.265, 0.030, size=n_players), 0.15, 0.40)
y_b = sim_rng2.binomial(at_bats, theta_b) / at_bats        # MLE
ybar_b = y_b.mean()
ss_b = np.sum((y_b - ybar_b) ** 2)
# Binomial sampling variance: theta(1-theta)/at_bats; use sigma^2 = pbar(1-pbar)/n
sigma2_b = ybar_b * (1 - ybar_b) / at_bats
c_b = max(0.0, 1 - (n_players - 3) * sigma2_b / ss_b)
y_js_b = ybar_b + c_b * (y_b - ybar_b)
y_gm_b = np.full(n_players, ybar_b)

mse = lambda est: np.mean((est - theta_b) ** 2)
mse_mle, mse_js, mse_gm = mse(y_b), mse(y_js_b), mse(y_gm_b)
wins_js = int(np.sum((y_js_b - theta_b) ** 2 < (y_b - theta_b) ** 2))

fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(10.5, 4.2))

# Left: the three estimators per player, ordered by truth
order_b = np.argsort(theta_b)
axes[0].plot(np.arange(n_players), theta_b[order_b], "k_",
             markersize=18, mew=2.5, label="True $\\theta$ (end of season)")
axes[0].scatter(np.arange(n_players), y_b[order_b], s=45, color="#888",
                marker="o", label="MLE (first 45 at-bats)")
axes[0].scatter(np.arange(n_players), y_js_b[order_b], s=45, color="#c0504d",
                marker="D", label="James–Stein")
axes[0].axhline(ybar_b, color="#1f6f8b", linestyle=":", lw=1, label=f"Grand mean ($\\bar y={ybar_b:.3f}$)")
axes[0].set_xlabel("Player (ordered by true average)")
axes[0].set_ylabel("Batting average")
axes[0].set_title("Three estimators on 18 simulated batters")
axes[0].legend(loc="upper left", fontsize=8.5, frameon=False)

# Right: aggregate squared-error comparison
labels = ["MLE", "James–Stein", "Grand mean"]
vals = [mse_mle, mse_js, mse_gm]
colors = ["#888", "#c0504d", "#1f6f8b"]
bars = axes[1].bar(labels, vals, color=colors, width=0.55)
for b, v in zip(bars, vals):
    axes[1].text(b.get_x() + b.get_width()/2, v + max(vals)*0.02,
                 f"{v*1e4:.1f}×10⁻⁴",
                 ha="center", va="bottom", fontsize=9)
axes[1].set_ylabel("Mean squared error vs. truth")
axes[1].set_title(f"JS beats MLE on aggregate; wins on {wins_js}/{n_players} individuals")
axes[1].set_ylim(0, max(vals) * 1.18)

plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()
```

The baseball example matters for genomic inference because the setup is structurally identical. Replace "players" with "genes," "batting averages" with "expression-level differences," and "end-of-season averages" with "true gene-level effects estimated from a much larger validation cohort," and the same mathematics applies. The procedures developed in the rest of this lecture — empirical Bayes, the two-groups model, the local FDR — are direct generalizations of the baseball calculation to the setting where the unknown parameters take a richer form than a single batting average.

This is the founding insight of empirical Bayes. When many parallel inferences are conducted under exchangeable conditions, the aggregate of observations carries information about any single one — information that classical, one-at-a-time procedures discard.

---

## Empirical Bayes: the data determines the prior

The Bayesian formulation of the parallel-inference problem assigns each $\theta_i$ a prior distribution and conditions on $y_i$ to obtain a posterior. The classical objection to Bayesian methods is that the prior must be specified by the analyst. In the high-dimensional setting, the objection loses its force: the population of $\theta_i$ is observable, in the sense that the histogram of $y_i$ is an estimate of the marginal distribution

$$f(y) = \int g(y \mid \theta)\, \pi(\theta)\, d\theta,$$

where $g$ is the sampling density and $\pi$ is the prior. With $d$ in the thousands, $f$ is estimated nonparametrically from the data, and $\pi$ is recovered by deconvolution. The data themselves determine the prior.

> "Letting the data determine the prior turns Bayesian inference from a subjective into a largely objective technique."
>
> — Efron, [*Large-Scale Inference*](https://doi.org/10.1017/CBO9780511761362), Ch. 1.

This is the empirical-Bayes program. In the multiple-testing setting, $\theta_i$ encodes whether gene $i$ is null or alternative, and the prior probability of being null is a single number $\pi_0$ that the histogram of $p$-values (or $z$-scores) makes immediately estimable.

---

## Why is the MLE still used? Estimation, prediction, and choice of loss

Stein's paradox does not say the sample mean is bad. It says the sample mean is inadmissible *under one specific loss function*: total squared error summed over all coordinates,

$$L(\hat{\boldsymbol\theta}, \boldsymbol\theta) = \sum_{i=1}^d (\hat\theta_i - \theta_i)^2.$$

Under this loss, the James–Stein estimator achieves strictly smaller risk than the MLE for every $\boldsymbol\theta$ whenever $d \geq 3$. The key qualifier is *strictly* — and the cost is that every individual coordinate is biased.

A short formulation captures the trade-off:

- *James–Stein* minimizes expected loss aggregated **over all coordinates jointly**.
- *MLE* minimizes expected loss for **this one coordinate individually**.

The two answer different questions, and which is appropriate depends on the inferential goal.

### Coordinatewise versus joint loss

If the analyst's loss is incurred *one coordinate at a time* — for instance, when each gene's effect size will be reported, cited, and validated independently of the others — the relevant loss is coordinatewise,

$$L_i(\hat\theta_i, \theta_i) = (\hat\theta_i - \theta_i)^2.$$

The MLE is admissible under this loss for each $i$ separately. The unbiasedness of $\hat\theta_i = y_i$ is a property the JS estimator does not have: shrinkage moves $\hat\theta_i^{\mathrm{JS}}$ systematically toward the grand mean, which improves the *sum* of squared errors but degrades any individual estimate. When the published claim is "the estimated effect for gene 4,237 is $\hat\theta_{4237}$" and that single estimate will be evaluated on its own merits, classical estimation remains the right tool.

The settings where JS-style shrinkage dominates are those in which the *aggregate* matters: a 30,000-gene effect-size vector, a 100,000-image classification problem, or a $p \gg n$ regression where the analyst cares about overall predictive performance rather than any single coefficient. In these settings, biasing individual coordinates is a fair trade for reducing the total error.

```{python}
#| label: fig-risk-curve
#| echo: false
#| fig-cap: "Risk curves for the MLE and James–Stein estimators as a function of the size of the true parameter vector, $\\|\\boldsymbol\\theta\\|^2/d$. Both axes are on a log scale; smaller is better. The MLE risk is flat at $\\sigma^2$ — the per-coordinate sampling variance — regardless of $\\boldsymbol\\theta$. The JS risk is *strictly smaller* for every value of $\\|\\boldsymbol\\theta\\|^2$, with the largest improvement when $\\|\\boldsymbol\\theta\\|$ is small (because then shrinking toward zero is approximately correct). The improvement narrows as $\\|\\boldsymbol\\theta\\|$ grows: JS still beats MLE but by less. Each point is averaged over 400 simulation runs at $d = 30$, $\\sigma^2 = 1$."
#| fig-height: 3.5
sim_rng5 = np.random.default_rng(3)
d_risk = 30
sigma2 = 1.0
sizes = np.logspace(-1, 2, 12)
n_reps = 400

mle_risks, js_risks = [], []
for s2 in sizes:
    # Draw theta with ||theta||^2 / d = s2 (so each theta_i has variance s2 around 0)
    rep_mle, rep_js = [], []
    for _ in range(n_reps):
        theta_r = sim_rng5.normal(0, np.sqrt(s2), size=d_risk)
        y_r = theta_r + sim_rng5.normal(0, np.sqrt(sigma2), size=d_risk)
        # MLE
        rep_mle.append(np.mean((y_r - theta_r) ** 2))
        # JS shrinking toward 0 (not the grand mean) for cleanest comparison
        ss_y = np.sum(y_r ** 2)
        c_r = max(0.0, 1 - (d_risk - 2) * sigma2 / ss_y)
        theta_js_r = c_r * y_r
        rep_js.append(np.mean((theta_js_r - theta_r) ** 2))
    mle_risks.append(np.mean(rep_mle))
    js_risks.append(np.mean(rep_js))

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(8.5, 3.5))
ax.plot(sizes, mle_risks, "o-", color="#444", lw=2, label="MLE")
ax.plot(sizes, js_risks, "s-", color="#c0504d", lw=2, label="James–Stein")
ax.set_xscale("log"); ax.set_yscale("log")
ax.set_xlabel(r"$\|\theta\|^2/d$ (size of true means; log scale)")
ax.set_ylabel("Mean squared error per coordinate (log scale)")
ax.set_title(f"JS dominates MLE under joint loss for every $\\|\\theta\\|^2$ at $d={d_risk}$")
ax.legend(loc="lower right", fontsize=9, frameon=False)
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()
```

### Estimation versus prediction

A second axis along which classical and empirical-Bayes methods diverge is the distinction between *estimation* and *prediction*.

- **Estimation** is the recovery of the parameters of the data-generating process — the true effect sizes, the true regression coefficients, the true cell-type proportions. Loss is measured in parameter space.
- **Prediction** is the forecasting of a future observation $\hat y_* = \mathbf{x}_*^\top \hat{\boldsymbol\beta}$. Loss is measured in observation space.

The bias-variance trade-off is more permissive in prediction than in estimation. An estimator can be substantially biased on individual coefficients yet predict well on aggregate, because errors in $\hat{\boldsymbol\beta}$ partially cancel when projected through the linear predictor onto $\hat y_*$. This is precisely the principle that ridge regression, the LASSO, and the elastic net (Lecture 4) exploit: their predictions can be more accurate than ordinary least squares while their coefficient estimates are visibly worse. James–Stein shrinkage operates by the same mechanism in the parallel-mean problem.

The takeaway: ask what is being predicted, against what loss, before choosing an estimator. A method that is the right tool for prediction may be the wrong tool for inference about a particular parameter, and conversely.

### How does this fit a decision framework?

Both the testing procedures of Lecture 1 and the estimators of this lecture are *decision rules* derived from explicit loss functions. The choices unify into a single sequence:

1. **Choose a loss.** What does an error cost, and is it incurred per test, per coordinate, in aggregate, or in prediction?
2. **Choose a framework.** Frequentist (no prior), Bayesian (specified prior), or empirical Bayes (prior estimated from the data)?
3. **Derive the optimal rule.** This is the test, estimator, or prediction procedure to use.

The decision framework introduced in Lecture 1 — Bonferroni, BH, $q$-values — selected procedures under FWER and FDR losses (loss per false positive, weighted by the criterion). The estimation framework of this lecture selects procedures under squared-error losses (per-coordinate or aggregate), with the local FDR appearing as a Bayesian decision rule under 0-1 loss on the null/alternative classification.

The empirical-Bayes machinery is not a replacement for classical estimation but a tool for the specific high-dimensional setting in which (a) the relevant loss is aggregate, (b) many parallel inferences are conducted, and (c) the prior is empirically estimable from the population of estimates. Below that scale — fewer tests, individual reports, confirmatory rather than exploratory inference — MLE remains both natural and (under the relevant loss) optimal.

---

## The two-groups model

[Efron (2004)](https://doi.org/10.1198/016214504000000089), elaborated in [Efron (2008)](https://doi.org/10.1214/07-STS236) and consolidated in [*Large-Scale Inference*](https://doi.org/10.1017/CBO9780511761362), formalizes the framework as a finite mixture. Each of the $m$ tests belongs to one of two latent groups: a null group, with prior probability $\pi_0$ and density $f_0$ (typically $N(0,1)$ for $z$-scores), and a non-null group, with prior probability $1-\pi_0$ and density $f_1$. The marginal density of the observed test statistics is the mixture

$$f(z) = \pi_0\, f_0(z) + (1-\pi_0)\, f_1(z).$$

The framework unifies the procedures of Lecture 1 and produces the local FDR as a corollary. With $m$ in the thousands, $f(z)$ can be estimated nonparametrically — for instance by a histogram or by Lindsey's method, which fits a Poisson regression to binned counts. Once $f$ is in hand, $\pi_0$ can be estimated from the central peak (where $f_0$ dominates) and $f_1$ recovered by subtraction.

```{python}
#| label: fig-two-groups-decomp
#| echo: false
#| fig-cap: "The two-groups mixture $f(z) = \\pi_0 f_0(z) + (1-\\pi_0) f_1(z)$. Simulated $z$-scores ($m=5{,}000$, $\\pi_0 = 0.85$) histogram in light blue. The blue density is the null contribution $\\pi_0 f_0(z)$; the orange density is the alternative contribution $(1-\\pi_0) f_1(z)$; the black curve is their sum, the mixture $f(z)$ that the histogram is estimating. Where the orange exceeds the blue (in the tails), the local FDR is below 0.5 and the corresponding tests are more likely non-null than null."
#| fig-height: 3.5
sim_rng3 = np.random.default_rng(7)
m_sim, pi0_sim = 5000, 0.85
m1 = int(round(m_sim * (1 - pi0_sim)))
z_null = sim_rng3.normal(0, 1, size=m_sim - m1)
z_alt = sim_rng3.normal(2.6, 1.1, size=m1)
z_all = np.concatenate([z_null, z_alt])

xs = np.linspace(-4.5, 6.5, 600)
f0_xs = norm.pdf(xs, 0, 1)
f1_xs = norm.pdf(xs, 2.6, 1.1)
fmix_xs = pi0_sim * f0_xs + (1 - pi0_sim) * f1_xs

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(9, 3.8))
ax.hist(z_all, bins=80, density=True, color="#cfe2f3", edgecolor="white",
        alpha=0.85, label=r"Observed $z$-scores")
ax.fill_between(xs, pi0_sim * f0_xs, color="#3b78cc", alpha=0.55,
                label=r"$\pi_0 f_0(z)$ (null component)")
ax.fill_between(xs, (1 - pi0_sim) * f1_xs, color="#e8923c", alpha=0.75,
                label=r"$(1-\pi_0) f_1(z)$ (alternative component)")
ax.plot(xs, fmix_xs, color="black", lw=1.8, label=r"Mixture $f(z)$")
ax.set_xlabel(r"$z$"); ax.set_ylabel("density")
ax.set_title(rf"Two-groups decomposition at $\pi_0 = {pi0_sim:.2f}$")
ax.legend(loc="upper right", fontsize=9, frameon=False)
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()
```

The entire pipeline is empirical Bayes: the aggregate data are used to estimate the prior $\pi_0$ and the alternative density $f_1$, and Bayes's theorem is then applied case by case.

The relationship to Storey's $\hat\pi_0$ (Lecture 1) is direct: that estimator counts $p$-values above a threshold $\lambda$ and divides by the expected number under the uniform null. It is the simplest possible plug-in estimator of the mixing weight in the two-groups model, with $f_0$ taken as uniform on $[0,1]$ (its form for $p$-values) and the central peak of $f$ approximated by its tail beyond $\lambda$.

---

## The local false discovery rate

The two-groups model affords a refinement that the tail-area FDR cannot: a per-test posterior probability of being null. Define

$$\mathrm{fdr}(z) = \Pr(\mathrm{null} \mid Z = z) = \frac{\pi_0\, f_0(z)}{f(z)}.$$

The local false discovery rate $\mathrm{fdr}(z)$ is, for any specific test with statistic $z$, the probability that the null hypothesis is true given that statistic. The tail-area FDR is its expectation over a rejection region:

$$\mathrm{FDR}(z_0) = \mathbb{E}\!\left[\mathrm{fdr}(Z) \mid Z \geq z_0\right] = \frac{\int_{z_0}^\infty \mathrm{fdr}(z)\, f(z)\, dz}{\int_{z_0}^\infty f(z)\, dz}.$$

A practical consequence: two tests with the same tail-area FDR can have very different local FDRs. A $z$-score at the boundary of a rejection region carries a high posterior probability of being null; a $z$-score deep in the tail carries a much lower one. The local FDR is the more informative summary at the level of individual tests, and the natural output of the estimation framework.

```{python}
#| label: fig-local-fdr-curve
#| echo: false
#| fig-cap: "The local false discovery rate $\\mathrm{fdr}(z) = \\pi_0 f_0(z)/f(z)$ for the simulated two-groups data of the previous figure. In the central peak, almost all of the density comes from the null component, so $\\mathrm{fdr}(z) \\approx 1$. In the tails, the alternative component dominates and $\\mathrm{fdr}(z)$ drops to small values. The horizontal dashed line at $\\mathrm{fdr} = 0.20$ is the common threshold for declaring a test non-null."
#| fig-height: 3.3
fdr_xs = (pi0_sim * f0_xs) / fmix_xs
fdr_xs = np.minimum(fdr_xs, 1.0)

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(9, 3.3))
ax.plot(xs, fdr_xs, color="#2a4d9c", lw=2)
ax.axhline(0.2, color="#c0504d", linestyle="--", lw=1.2,
           label=r"$\mathrm{fdr}(z) = 0.20$ threshold")
# Mark the z-scores at which fdr crosses 0.20
above = fdr_xs > 0.20
crossings_idx = np.where(np.diff(above.astype(int)) != 0)[0]
for k in crossings_idx:
    z_cross = xs[k]
    ax.axvline(z_cross, color="#888", linestyle=":", lw=0.9)
    ax.text(z_cross, 0.92, f"$z = {z_cross:+.2f}$",
            rotation=90, va="top", ha="right", fontsize=8, color="#444")

ax.set_xlabel(r"$z$"); ax.set_ylabel(r"$\mathrm{fdr}(z)$")
ax.set_title("Local FDR curve: posterior probability of being null at each $z$")
ax.set_ylim(0, 1.05)
ax.legend(loc="upper center", fontsize=9, frameon=False)
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()
```

The decision rule "reject when $\mathrm{fdr}(z) < c$" for some threshold $c$ (often $c = 0.2$ in genomics) is equivalent to applying a Bayesian decision rule with the empirical-Bayes posterior, and produces results that closely track BH for genes in the tails of the distribution while diverging — informatively — for genes near the rejection boundary.

---

## The two-groups model on the Golub data

The procedure can be made concrete by applying it to the dataset that has accompanied every lecture so far: the 3,051 gene-level $t$-statistics from Golub's ALL/AML comparison (Lab 1). Convert each $t$ to a $z$-score via $z_i = \Phi^{-1}(1 - p_i/2)\cdot \mathrm{sign}(\hat\beta_i)$, where the sign is taken from the direction of the mean difference. The histogram of these 3,051 $z$-scores shows the structure the two-groups model is designed to capture: a central peak roughly resembling $N(0,1)$, and heavy tails on both sides.

```{python}
#| label: fig-golub-zhist
#| echo: false
#| fig-cap: "The histogram of 3,051 gene-level $z$-scores from the Golub ALL/AML comparison (light blue) with the standard-normal density overlaid (red dashed line). The central peak is approximately $N(0,1)$ but the tails are visibly heavier — the signature of the non-null genes."
#| fig-height: 3.5
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(9, 3.5))
ax.hist(golub_z, bins=80, density=True, color="#cfe2f3", edgecolor="white",
        alpha=0.85, label=r"Observed $z$-scores ($m=3{,}051$)")
z_grid = np.linspace(golub_z.min() - 0.5, golub_z.max() + 0.5, 500)
ax.plot(z_grid, norm.pdf(z_grid, 0, 1), color="#c0504d", lw=2, linestyle="--",
        label=r"Theoretical null $N(0,1)$")
ax.set_xlabel(r"$z$"); ax.set_ylabel("density")
ax.set_title("Golub $z$-scores: central peak + heavy tails")
ax.legend(loc="upper right", fontsize=9, frameon=False)
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()
```

### Estimating the mixture

Fit $\hat f(z)$ nonparametrically — for instance, by kernel density estimation or Lindsey's Poisson regression on binned counts. Assume the theoretical null $f_0(z) = \phi(z)$ (the standard normal density). Estimate

$$\hat\pi_0 = \frac{\#\{p_i > \lambda\}}{m(1-\lambda)},\quad \lambda = 0.5,$$

following Storey's method introduced in Lecture 1. In the Golub data, $\hat\pi_0$ comes out near 0.6: roughly 60 percent of the 3,051 genes are estimated to be null, leaving an effective $\hat m_1 \approx 1{,}200$ non-null genes. The proportion non-null is large because ALL and AML are genuinely different diseases — the transcriptomic differences between them are substantial, and that biological reality is visible in the histogram as a flat region whose height is well below one.

```{python}
#| label: fig-golub-twogroups
#| echo: false
#| fig-cap: "Two-groups decomposition fit to the Golub $z$-scores. The Storey-style estimator gives $\\hat\\pi_0 \\approx 0.6$. The null contribution $\\hat\\pi_0\\,\\phi(z)$ (blue) matches the central peak; the alternative contribution (orange) is recovered by subtracting the null from a kernel density estimate of the marginal $\\hat f(z)$ (black curve)."
#| fig-height: 3.5
# Storey's pi0 estimate
lam = 0.5
pi0_golub = float(np.sum(golub_pvals > lam) / (n_genes * (1 - lam)))
pi0_golub = min(pi0_golub, 1.0)

# KDE of marginal f-hat
kde = stats.gaussian_kde(golub_z, bw_method=0.20)
z_grid = np.linspace(-6, 6, 400)
f_hat = kde(z_grid)
f0_g = norm.pdf(z_grid, 0, 1)
f1_contrib = np.clip(f_hat - pi0_golub * f0_g, 0, None)

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(9, 3.5))
ax.hist(golub_z, bins=80, density=True, color="#cfe2f3", edgecolor="white",
        alpha=0.75, label=r"Observed $z$-scores")
ax.fill_between(z_grid, pi0_golub * f0_g, color="#3b78cc", alpha=0.55,
                label=rf"$\hat\pi_0\, \phi(z),\ \hat\pi_0={pi0_golub:.2f}$")
ax.fill_between(z_grid, f1_contrib, color="#e8923c", alpha=0.7,
                label=r"$(1-\hat\pi_0)\,\hat f_1(z)$ (recovered)")
ax.plot(z_grid, f_hat, color="black", lw=1.5, label=r"$\hat f(z)$ (KDE)")
ax.set_xlabel(r"$z$"); ax.set_ylabel("density")
ax.set_title("Two-groups fit on Golub")
ax.legend(loc="upper right", fontsize=9, frameon=False)
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()
```

### Computing the local FDR

With $\hat\pi_0$, $f_0$, and $\hat f$ in hand,

$$\widehat{\mathrm{fdr}}(z) = \frac{\hat\pi_0\, f_0(z)}{\hat f(z)}.$$

The curve $\widehat{\mathrm{fdr}}(z)$ is close to 1 for $z$ near zero (almost all of the central peak is null), drops steeply as $|z|$ increases, and approaches zero in the tails. Plotting $\widehat{\mathrm{fdr}}(z)$ as a function of $z$ alongside the histogram produces the diagnostic image that practitioners learn to read: the histogram tells you *what* signal is present; the local FDR curve tells you *which $z$-scores correspond to genes you should believe*.

```{python}
#| label: fig-golub-localfdr
#| echo: false
#| fig-cap: "Local FDR curve $\\widehat{\\mathrm{fdr}}(z) = \\hat\\pi_0\\,\\phi(z)/\\hat f(z)$ for the Golub data. Near $z = 0$, $\\widehat{\\mathrm{fdr}}(z) \\approx 1$ — almost all of the density is null. The curve drops to 0.20 around $|z| \\approx 2$ and toward zero in the tails. Vertical dashed lines mark the rejection thresholds at the conventional cutoff $\\widehat{\\mathrm{fdr}}(z) < 0.20$."
#| fig-height: 3.3
fdr_grid = np.clip(pi0_golub * f0_g / np.maximum(f_hat, 1e-12), 0, 1)
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(9, 3.3))
ax.plot(z_grid, fdr_grid, color="#2a4d9c", lw=2)
ax.axhline(0.20, color="#c0504d", linestyle="--", lw=1.2,
           label=r"$\widehat{\mathrm{fdr}}(z) = 0.20$ threshold")
above = fdr_grid > 0.20
crossings = np.where(np.diff(above.astype(int)) != 0)[0]
for k in crossings:
    z_cross = z_grid[k]
    ax.axvline(z_cross, color="#888", linestyle=":", lw=0.9)
    ax.text(z_cross, 0.93, f"$z={z_cross:+.2f}$", rotation=90,
            va="top", ha="right", fontsize=8, color="#444")
ax.set_xlabel(r"$z$"); ax.set_ylabel(r"$\widehat{\mathrm{fdr}}(z)$")
ax.set_title("Local FDR on the Golub data")
ax.set_ylim(0, 1.05); ax.set_xlim(-6, 6)
ax.legend(loc="lower center", fontsize=9, frameon=False)
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()
```

### Comparison with Lab 1's BH list

Lab 1 reports the Golub gene list obtained by applying BH at FDR $= 0.05$. That list contains a few hundred genes — those with the smallest $p$-values, up to the BH cutoff. Applying the local FDR rule $\widehat{\mathrm{fdr}}(z) < 0.20$ to the same data produces a list of comparable size, with substantial overlap. The two lists differ at the margin: a gene with $z$ just past the BH cutoff but with a local FDR of 0.18 is more confidently non-null than a gene at the BH boundary with a local FDR of 0.35. The local FDR gives the analyst a per-gene confidence that the tail-area BH cannot. Lab 2 walks through this comparison explicitly.

```{python}
#| label: fig-bh-vs-localfdr
#| echo: false
#| fig-cap: "Discovery-set comparison on the Golub data. Bonferroni at $\\alpha = 0.05$ produces a small list dominated by the very tails of the distribution. BH at FDR $=0.05$ (Lecture 1) produces a much larger list. The local-FDR rule $\\widehat{\\mathrm{fdr}}(z) < 0.20$ yields a list of comparable size to BH, with substantial overlap but informative differences at the margins."
#| fig-height: 3.3
from statsmodels.stats.multitest import multipletests
# Per-gene local fdr by interpolating the curve at each observed z
fdr_per_gene = np.clip(pi0_golub * norm.pdf(golub_z, 0, 1) / np.maximum(kde(golub_z), 1e-12), 0, 1)
bonf_thresh = 0.05 / n_genes
n_bonf = int(np.sum(golub_pvals < bonf_thresh))
_, p_bh, _, _ = multipletests(golub_pvals, alpha=0.05, method="fdr_bh")
n_bh = int(np.sum(p_bh <= 0.05))
n_lfdr = int(np.sum(fdr_per_gene < 0.20))

labels = ["Bonferroni\nα=0.05", "BH\nFDR=0.05", "Local FDR\nfdr<0.20"]
counts = [n_bonf, n_bh, n_lfdr]
colors = ["#888", "#3b78cc", "#c0504d"]
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(9, 3.3))
bars = ax.bar(labels, counts, color=colors, width=0.55)
for b, c in zip(bars, counts):
    ax.text(b.get_x() + b.get_width()/2, c + max(counts)*0.02, f"{c}",
            ha="center", va="bottom", fontsize=11)
ax.set_ylabel("Genes called significant")
ax.set_title("Three discovery rules on the Golub data")
ax.set_ylim(0, max(counts) * 1.18)
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()
```

### What the histogram does *not* tell you

It tells you nothing about *which* genes are non-null; only how many, and at what $z$-score they are likely to live. The biological interpretation of any individual significant gene — whether it is in a meaningful pathway, whether it is independently validated, whether the direction of effect makes mechanistic sense — is a separate step that no statistical procedure can supply. The role of the empirical-Bayes machinery is to produce a list short enough to validate experimentally and confidently enough labeled to be worth the effort.

---

## Empirical Bayes in practice: limma's variance shrinkage

The two-groups model applies empirical Bayes to a *binary* parameter (null versus alternative). The same logic applies to continuous parameters, and the most widely used instance of it in genomics is the variance shrinkage performed by the `limma` package ([Smyth, 2004](https://doi.org/10.2202/1544-6115.1027)).

### The problem limma solves

Consider a microarray experiment with $n$ samples and $p$ genes — for the Golub data, $n = 72$ and $p = 3{,}051$. A standard analysis applies a two-sample $t$-test gene by gene, comparing groups A and B. For gene $g$ the $t$-statistic is

$$t_g = \frac{\bar y_{g,A} - \bar y_{g,B}}{s_g\, \sqrt{1/n_A + 1/n_B}},$$

where $s_g$ is the sample standard deviation of gene $g$'s expression values across all samples. The problem is that $s_g$, estimated from at most a few dozen observations, is itself extremely noisy. A gene whose true variance happens to be small will, by chance, occasionally produce an $s_g$ that is even smaller — and the resulting $t$-statistic will be artificially large. In a high-dimensional experiment with thousands of genes, the gene at the top of the list ordered by $t$-statistic is more likely to be a gene with an underestimated variance than a gene with a genuinely large effect.

### The shrinkage estimator

The limma fix is to treat the gene-level variances $\sigma_g^2$ as draws from a shared prior distribution — an inverse-chi-squared with parameters $(d_0, s_0^2)$ — and to update each gene's variance estimate by conjugacy. The posterior mean of $\sigma_g^2$ given the observed $s_g^2$ is

$$\tilde s_g^2 = \frac{d_0\, s_0^2 + d_g\, s_g^2}{d_0 + d_g},$$

a weighted average of the individual sample variance $s_g^2$ (with $d_g$ residual degrees of freedom) and the prior estimate $s_0^2$ (with $d_0$ degrees of freedom). The prior parameters $(d_0, s_0^2)$ are estimated from the *population* of observed sample variances — empirical Bayes, exactly as in the two-groups model.

The moderated $t$-statistic uses $\tilde s_g$ in place of $s_g$:

$$\tilde t_g = \frac{\bar y_{g,A} - \bar y_{g,B}}{\tilde s_g\, \sqrt{1/n_A + 1/n_B}}.$$

The moderated statistic is more conservative for genes with anomalously small $s_g$ (because $\tilde s_g > s_g$ in that case) and more sensitive for genes with anomalously large $s_g$ (because $\tilde s_g < s_g$ then). The reference distribution under the null is a $t$ with $d_0 + d_g$ degrees of freedom rather than $d_g$ alone — additional degrees of freedom from the borrowing.

```{python}
#| label: fig-limma-shrinkage
#| echo: false
#| fig-cap: "limma-style empirical-Bayes variance shrinkage on the Golub data. Each point is one of the 3,051 genes. The horizontal axis is the raw gene-level standard deviation $s_g$ (the maximum-likelihood estimate); the vertical axis is the moderated estimate $\\tilde s_g$ that shrinks $s_g$ toward the population prior. The diagonal is the identity (no shrinkage). Genes with anomalously small $s_g$ are pulled *up* (away from zero, increasing the implicit $t$-statistic denominator and the moderated test is more conservative), while genes with anomalously large $s_g$ are pulled *down*. The shrinkage prevents spurious significance driven by a coincidentally small $s_g$."
#| fig-height: 3.6
# Per-gene sample SDs (pooled across the two groups)
all_data = expr.loc[:, all_mask].values
aml_data = expr.loc[:, aml_mask].values
n_a, n_b = all_data.shape[1], aml_data.shape[1]
ss_within = ((all_data - all_data.mean(axis=1, keepdims=True))**2).sum(axis=1) \
          + ((aml_data - aml_data.mean(axis=1, keepdims=True))**2).sum(axis=1)
d_g = n_a + n_b - 2
s_g = np.sqrt(ss_within / d_g)

# Empirical-Bayes prior on sigma^2 ~ inverse-chi-squared via method-of-moments
# (limma fits this by maximum likelihood; this approximation suffices for plotting)
log_s2 = np.log(s_g**2)
mean_log = log_s2.mean()
var_log = log_s2.var()
# Use trigamma inversion approximately: d0 such that trigamma(d0/2) approx var_log + ...
# For the figure, use a generic shrinkage strength d0 = 4 and s0^2 = exp(mean_log)
d0 = 4.0
s0_sq = float(np.exp(mean_log))

s_tilde = np.sqrt((d0 * s0_sq + d_g * s_g**2) / (d0 + d_g))

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(8.5, 3.6))
mx = float(np.percentile(np.concatenate([s_g, s_tilde]), 99))
ax.plot([0, mx], [0, mx], color="#888", lw=1, linestyle="--", label="No shrinkage")
ax.axhline(np.sqrt(s0_sq), color="#c0504d", lw=1.2, linestyle=":",
           label=rf"Prior $\sqrt{{s_0^2}} \approx {np.sqrt(s0_sq):.2f}$")
ax.scatter(s_g, s_tilde, s=4, alpha=0.5, color="#2a4d9c", rasterized=True)
ax.set_xlabel(r"Raw gene-level SD $s_g$")
ax.set_ylabel(r"Moderated SD $\tilde s_g$")
ax.set_title(rf"Variance shrinkage pulls $s_g$ toward the prior ($d_0={d0:.0f},\ s_0^2={s0_sq:.2f}$)")
ax.set_xlim(0, mx); ax.set_ylim(0, mx)
ax.legend(loc="upper left", fontsize=9, frameon=False)
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()
```

### Why this matters in practice

The empirical-Bayes shrinkage of variance estimates is the single most important methodological improvement in microarray-era differential expression analysis. Genes that would have been falsely declared significant on the basis of a coincidentally small $s_g$ are no longer. Genes whose effects are genuine but whose $s_g$ happens to be large are not penalized as severely. The net effect on the discovery list — at constant FDR — is a substantial reduction in both false positives and false negatives, and the procedure has become the default in RNA-seq pipelines (via the related `voom` and `edgeR` packages) as well as in the original microarray setting.

The structural identity with the rest of this lecture is the point. The two-groups model uses the histogram of $z$-scores to learn the prior probability that any individual gene is null. `limma` uses the histogram of *variance estimates* to learn the prior on gene-level variance. Both are empirical Bayes; both produce more accurate per-test inference by pooling information across the population of tests. Lab 2 and the homework apply `limma::eBayes` to the Golub data directly; this section provides the conceptual justification.

---

## A cautionary case: the Singh prostate-cancer reanalysis

The two-groups model assumes that $f_0$ is known. In a textbook calculation, $z$-scores derived from $t$-statistics with appropriate degrees of freedom are taken to be $N(0,1)$ under the null. Real data frequently fail this assumption — and the failure is diagnosable from the same histogram that everything else in this lecture is read from.

### The original analysis

[Singh and colleagues (2002)](https://doi.org/10.1016/S1535-6108(02)00030-2) profiled 6,033 genes by microarray on 102 prostate samples: 50 from histologically normal prostate tissue and 52 from prostate-cancer specimens. The original analysis applied a two-sample $t$-test gene by gene and reported the genes that crossed a conventional significance threshold. The natural empirical-Bayes follow-up — taken up by Efron in [*Large-Scale Inference*](https://doi.org/10.1017/CBO9780511761362), Ch. 6 — is to convert the $t$-statistics to $z$-scores, fit the two-groups mixture, and report the local FDR.

### The diagnostic

When the $z$-scores are plotted as a histogram, the central peak should be visually indistinguishable from a $N(0,1)$ density — that is what the theoretical null says it should be. In the Singh data the peak is visibly wider. The empirical histogram is unimodal and approximately symmetric around zero, but with a standard deviation closer to 1.1–1.2 than to 1.0; in [Efron's analysis](https://doi.org/10.1198/016214504000000089), the maximum-likelihood empirical null estimated from the central portion of the data has

$$\hat\delta_0 \approx -0.02, \qquad \hat\sigma_0 \approx 1.10.$$

A $\hat\sigma_0$ of 1.10 may sound small, but its consequences for tail-area inference are large. The theoretical $N(0,1)$ density assigns probability $2 \cdot 10^{-7}$ to the event $|Z| \geq 5$; under $N(0, 1.10^2)$ the same event has probability $4 \cdot 10^{-6}$ — a twentyfold inflation. Genes that look like extreme outliers under the theoretical null are well within the body of the empirical null.

```{python}
#| label: fig-empirical-null
#| echo: false
#| fig-cap: "Simulation mimicking the Singh prostate-cancer histogram. 5,500 simulated null $z$-scores are drawn from $N(0, 1.10^2)$ (matching Efron's empirical-null fit) and 500 alternatives from $N(2.8, 1)$. The light-blue histogram is the observed mixture. The red dashed curve is the assumed theoretical null $N(0,1)$; it is too narrow to match the central peak. The blue solid curve is the empirical null fit to the central portion of the histogram; it matches. Applying the theoretical null produces many more false discoveries than applying the empirical null."
#| fig-height: 3.5
sim_rng4 = np.random.default_rng(2024)
m_sim2, m1_sim2 = 6000, 500
z_null_sim = sim_rng4.normal(0, 1.10, size=m_sim2 - m1_sim2)
z_alt_sim = sim_rng4.normal(2.8, 1.0, size=m1_sim2)
z_obs = np.concatenate([z_null_sim, z_alt_sim])

# Fit empirical null to the central portion (|z| < 2)
central = z_obs[np.abs(z_obs) < 2]
delta0_hat, sigma0_hat = float(central.mean()), float(central.std())

xs2 = np.linspace(-5, 6.5, 500)
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(9, 3.5))
ax.hist(z_obs, bins=80, density=True, color="#cfe2f3", edgecolor="white",
        alpha=0.85, label="Observed $z$-scores")
ax.plot(xs2, norm.pdf(xs2, 0, 1), color="#c0504d", lw=2, linestyle="--",
        label="Theoretical null $N(0,1)$ — too narrow")
ax.plot(xs2, norm.pdf(xs2, delta0_hat, sigma0_hat), color="#2a4d9c", lw=2,
        label=fr"Empirical null $N({delta0_hat:+.2f},\,{sigma0_hat:.2f}^2)$ — matches")
ax.set_xlabel("$z$"); ax.set_ylabel("density")
ax.set_title("When the empirical null is wider than $N(0,1)$")
ax.legend(loc="upper right", fontsize=9, frameon=False)
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()
```

### The consequence

Under the theoretical $N(0,1)$ null, the local FDR identifies a relatively large set of "non-null" genes at $\mathrm{fdr}(z) < 0.20$. Under the empirical null — fit to the central peak of the histogram — that list shrinks substantially: the same genes are now consistent with the wider null and no longer surpass the FDR threshold. The original analysis, conducted under the implicit assumption of a unit-variance null, *overstated discovery*. The empirical-null analysis returns a smaller, more credible list.

The mechanism is the one introduced earlier in the lecture: unobserved sources of variation — batch effects, latent population structure, correlation among tests — inflate the effective null variance. The empirical null acknowledges this by fitting $f_0$ to the central portion of the histogram rather than assuming $N(0,1)$.

### The protocol

The diagnostic is mechanical and should be part of any local-FDR workflow:

1. Convert test statistics to $z$-scores.
2. Estimate $\hat f(z)$ nonparametrically (Lindsey's method or a smooth histogram).
3. Fit $f_0(z) = N(\hat\delta_0, \hat\sigma_0^2)$ to the central portion of the observed distribution (typically $|z| < 2$ or via a maximum-likelihood fit to the central peak).
4. Check whether $\hat\sigma_0 \approx 1$ and $\hat\delta_0 \approx 0$.
5. If yes, the theoretical null is adequate; if no, use the empirical null in place of $N(0,1)$ to compute $\mathrm{fdr}(z)$.

The R package `locfdr` automates the procedure; Python implementations follow the same recipe.

The Singh case is not an isolated curiosity. In Efron's analyses of multiple microarray and proteomic datasets, $\hat\sigma_0 > 1$ is the common outcome rather than the exception. The empirical null is the most important practical lesson of the estimation framework, and the same picture that estimates $\pi_0$ and $f_1$ also exposes whether the assumed null was correct in the first place.

---

## When does the framework apply?

The empirical-Bayes machinery requires three conditions. The number of tests $m$ must be large enough — at least several hundred, ideally several thousand — to support nonparametric estimation of $f(z)$. The tests must be exchangeable, in the sense that they share the same null distribution and similar noise structure; mixing fundamentally different measurements (gene expression with DNA methylation, say) violates this requirement. Finally, the two-groups mixture must be a reasonable approximation to the data-generating process.

Failures of these conditions are diagnosable. Small $m$ produces noisy estimates of $f$ and, consequently, of $\hat\pi_0$ and the local FDR. Strong correlation among tests can distort the empirical null; in the worst case, no decomposition of $f$ into a null and an alternative is interpretable. Heterogeneity across tests can render the null density itself ill-defined.

A practical decision framework, scaled to the number of tests and the inferential goal:

- Few tests (1–20): the FWER, controlled by Bonferroni or Holm, is the right target.
- Many tests with confirmatory goals: FWER procedures remain appropriate.
- Many tests for discovery (100–1,000): Benjamini–Hochberg or Storey's $q$-values.
- Very many tests (1,000+): Efron's two-groups model and local FDR.

---

## Summary and bridge to Day 2

This lecture replaced the testing perspective of Lecture 1 with an estimation perspective. The histogram that diagnosed signal in Lecture 1 became, in this lecture, the basis for estimating the proportion of nulls, the alternative density, and a per-test posterior probability of being null. Five claims structure the content. First, the parallel-estimation problem created by high-dimensional data favors estimators that depend on the aggregate of observations rather than on any individual one (Stein, James–Stein). Second, empirical Bayes operationalizes this insight by estimating the prior from the data. Third, Efron's two-groups model is the standard formalization: $f = \pi_0 f_0 + (1-\pi_0) f_1$. Fourth, the local false discovery rate is the per-test posterior probability of being null and is the most informative summary at the level of individual tests. Fifth, the assumed null distribution $f_0$ should always be checked against the empirical one; the Singh prostate-cancer reanalysis is the standard cautionary case.

Lab 2 applies these procedures to the Golub dataset: $\hat\pi_0$, $q$-values, the two-groups mixture, the local FDR, and an empirical-null fit. Day 2 continues the estimation theme in different geometric form: dimension reduction (Lecture 3) estimates low-dimensional structure embedded in high-dimensional measurements; penalized regression (Lecture 4) estimates regression coefficients in the $p \gg n$ regime where ordinary least squares fails by construction. The thread is continuous — the histogram of $p$-values, the manifold of expression profiles, and the sparse regression coefficient vector are all objects to be estimated from the same kind of data — and the workshop's emphasis on critical evaluation persists throughout.
